retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When divided on the mid-line sagittal plane, these teeth are organized into the right and left halves. Incisors and canines can be classified further as "anterior teeth" and molars and premolars as "posterior teeth." The primary teeth erupt during the first year and into the third years of life and are then exfoliated(shed) between the ages of 6 and 12. Since primary teeth are shed, as some trees shed their leaves, they are also referred to as deciduous teeth, which are eventually replaced by the adult permanent dentition. [2] Primary teeth are labeled in the following classification system and are designated in a clockwise direction: A: Right maxillary 2nd molar B: Right maxillary 1st molar C: Right maxillary canine D: Right maxillary lateral incisor E: Right maxillary central incisor F: Left maxillary central incisor G: Left maxillary lateral incisor H: Left maxillary canine I: Left maxillary 1st molar J: Left maxillary 2nd molar T: Right mandibular 2nd molar

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: S: Right mandibular 1st molar R: Right mandibular canine Q: Right mandibular lateral incisor P: Right mandibular central incisor O: Left mandibular central incisor N: Left mandibular lateral incisor M: Left mandibular canine L: Left mandibular 1st molar K: Left mandibular 2nd molar Eruption(appearance) and exfoliation(shedding) of the primary dentition occur in the following order:

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary and Mandibular Dental Arch
retrieved_docs.content: The permanent, succedaneous teeth in adults consist of 16 teeth in the maxilla and 16 teeth in the mandible. The universal numbering system for permanent teeth assigns numbers to each tooth from 1 to 32. Tooth #1 is the right maxillary third molar followed sequentially by numbers 2-15 across the maxilla until reaching the left maxillary third molar, tooth #16. Numbering continues down to the mandible, with tooth #17 being the left mandibular third molar followed by 18-31 proceeding sequentially across the mandible until reaching the right mandibular third molar, which is tooth #32. [7] [8] There are alternative numbering systems.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the mandible, central and lateral incisors have single roots, with central incisors often lacking apical curvature. [6] If present in either tooth, apical curvature tends to be distally inclined. [6] Mandibular canines usually present with single roots, but one unique difference between maxillary and mandibular canines is that mandibular canines may have bifurcated roots similar to those seen in maxillary first premolars. [6] The mandibular first and second premolars typically present with single roots but may have bifurcated roots in rare situations. The root tips are often inclined distally, and the mandibular second premolar's root is usually longer than the first premolar's. [7] The mandibular first and second molars usually have two distally tilted roots: a mesial and a distal root. The mandibular second molar's roots are usually shorter with less splay than the first molar roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary and Mandibular Dental Arch
retrieved_docs.content: Adult dentition consists of 2 central incisors, 2 lateral incisors, 2 canines, 2 first premolars, 2-second premolars, 2 first molars, 2-second molars, and 2 third molars, both in the maxilla and mandible. The teeth are secured within the alveolar ridges of the maxilla and mandible. Dentition, whether native or restored, is integral to masticatory function. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Eruption Patterns
retrieved_docs.content: The first permanent teeth that emerge in the oral cavity are the maxillary and mandibular first molars (see Image. First Sign of Right Incisor Breakthrough). These teeth typically emerge around age 6 and are called 6-year molars. These molars erupt distal to the deciduous second molars and are accompanied or preceded by the exfoliation of the deciduous mandibular central incisors. The permanent mandibular incisors typically erupt in individuals aged 6 to 7, followed by the permanent maxillary incisors between the ages of 7 and 9. The permanent anterior teeth develop lingual or palatal to the deciduous teeth. In contrast, the permanent premolars grow in the spaces between and beneath the roots of the deciduous molars they replace.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Humans have two sets of teeth during their lifetime: the initial deciduous (primary) teeth and the successive permanent (secondary) teeth. [1] There are typically 20 deciduous teeth divided evenly across the maxilla and mandible. The deciduous teeth eventually exfoliate and are replaced by 32 permanent teeth: 16 in the maxilla and 16 in the mandible. Permanent teeth are classified as incisors, canines, premolars, and molars, while primary teeth do not include premolars.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Maxillary first molars typically have two buccal roots and one larger palatal root. [4] Roots may splay and have variable curvature. [4] Both the maxillary first and second molars have distal root inclinations, with the second molar often to a greater degree than the first. The second molar also traditionally has three roots that are usually less splayed than the first molar. [5] In some cases, the roots may fuse on the buccal aspect. The maxillary third molar has the most significant anatomical variability of all teeth. The roots of the maxillary third molar tend to be fused and shorter than the maxillary first and second molars. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Winter classification
retrieved_docs.content: The Winter classification describes mandibular 3rd molars based on the angulation of the crown with respect to the long axis of the 2nd molar. The Winter classification is as follows: Vertical Mesioangular Distoangular Horizontal Buccolingual Inverted Other Orientation Horizontal and distoangular impactions are more difficult to extract, as the path of delivery of the tooth is obstructed by overlying bone and the ascending ramus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The mandibular first molars (tooth S, L) have four cusps: two buccal and two lingual. These include the distobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and mesiobuccal cusps. The occlusal surface is narrow secondary to mesiobuccal and mesiolingual cusp convergence. There are three pits.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The two ossified right and left halves of the mandible remain separated by connective tissue until closer to birth when small mental bone(s) (1 or 2) ossify. These mental bones ultimately fuse with the mandible creating a mental protuberance and the unified mandibular bone. [11] Mesenchymal cells from the first branchial arch give rise to the muscles of mastication. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The primary dentition should not be confused with the permanent dentition, composed of 32 teeth with 16 teeth in each arch. Each of the primary molars is replaced by permanent, premolar teeth. There are three permanent molars in each quadrant that are not succeeded by primary teeth and may be referred to as non-succedaneous teeth. The arrival of these teeth, 12 in total, gives permanent dentition 32 teeth compared to the 20 teeth of primary dentition.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Variation in static occlusion seen in the general population can be further described using various classifications. In 1899, Edward Angle introduced his classification of occlusion. [17] This system is based on normal occlusion being determined by the relationship between the maxillary and mandibular first molar as below. Angle’s Classifications [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The maxillary first molars (tooth B, I) morphologically resemble both a molar and a premolar. Its occlusal surface consists of the mesiobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and distobuccal cusps. The largest cusp is the mesiolingual cusp, and it also represents the sharpest cusp. The distolingual cusp is small and rounded in morphology. The buccal surface is smooth, and the three roots are long and widely spread. Of note, the primary 1st molar has a less pronounced mesiobuccal cusp when compared to the permanent 1st molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of one or more of these radiographic features indicates the need for further imaging by way of CBCT, which can provide further information on the relationship between the lower third molar and the IAN.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth eruption is the process of a developing tooth penetrating oral soft tissue to enter the oral cavity. The eruption occurs when the mineralization and formation of the crown are mostly complete but before the full formation of the roots. When erupting teeth are no longer impeded by oral soft tissue, they erupt rapidly and are fully erupted within six months as long as there is space for them. Usually, the first primary teeth to erupt are the mandibular central incisors between 6 to 10 months of age. Next, the primary maxillary central incisors and lateral incisors erupt between 8 to 13 months of age. The primary mandibular lateral incisors erupt between 10 to 16 months of age. The primary canines erupt between 16 to 23 months of age. The primary first molars erupt between 13 to 19 months of age. The primary second molars erupt between 23 to 33 months of age. The primary central incisors shed between 6 to 7 years of age and are succeeded by the eruption of permanent mandibular central incisors at 6 to 7 years of age and maxillary central incisors at 7 to 8 years of age. The primary lateral incisors shed between 7 to 8 years of age and are succeeded by the eruption of permanent mandibular lateral incisors between 7 to 8 years of age and maxillary lateral incisors between 8 to 9 years of age. The primary canines shed between 9 to 12 years of age and are succeeded by the eruption of permanent mandibular canines between 9 to 10 years of age and maxillary canines between 11 to 12 years of age. The primary first molars shed between 9 to 11 years of age and are succeeded by the eruption of permanent mandibular first premolars between 10 to 12 years of age and maxillary first premolars between 10 to 11 years of age. The primary second molars shed between 10 to 12 years of age and are succeeded by the eruption of permanent second premolars between 10 to 12 years of age. The permanent first molars erupt between 6 to 7 years of age. The permanent second molars erupt between 11 to 13 years of age. The permanent third molars erupt between 17 to 21 years of age if there is room for them. [1] [8] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: The cervical canal structure is anatomically situated within the structure of the internal os, with the internal os serving as the parent structure that encompasses the cervical canal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is recognized as a manifestation of head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق). This relationship indicates that the spread of malignant cells to the lymph nodes within the head and neck region is a specific form of head and neck cancer, highlighting its relevance for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2575
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck displays morphological characteristics that are associated with السرطان النقيلي, indicating that the histopathologic appearance of the metastatic tumor in cervical lymph nodes reflects the typical morphology of السرطان النقيلي and aids in accurate diagnosis and appropriate management of patients with head‑and‑neck metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: The head of the first metatarsal bone is a distinct anatomical region of the foot that is defined by laterality, indicating that it can be identified as belonging to either the left or right side.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a type of الورم السرطاني (malignant tumor) and its associated morphology is described by الورم النقيلي (metastatic cancer). This indicates that the spread of carcinoma to the lymph nodes in the head and neck region represents a malignant neoplasm whose pathological features correspond to metastatic disease, a distinction that is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.
